title: Anne Marie Green
text:
Hello!
I’m Anne Marie Green, and I’m a third-year Ph.D. student studying American Politics at the University of California, Berkeley. My research interests are in political behavior, identity, policy representation, and symbolic politics. I study representation in politics, with a specific focus on how voters feel socially and symbolically represented. My work focuses on symbolic policies, or policies that are primarily about meaning-making or recognition than the provision of material resources. I use a variety of methods, including original surveys, LLM qualitative coding, text analysis, and ethnography.
Before coming to Berkeley, I graduated summa cum laude from Boston College with a degree in Political Science, and spent two years as a Pre-Doctoral Fellow in behavioral science at Harvard Business School.
Outside of my research, I enjoy writing about politics and other topics on my substack and taking photos of American political life.
You can read more about my current research here, or find my CV here.

title: Research
text:
Legislating Symbols: Does Status Threat Cause Voters to Demand Symbolic Representation? 
      
      
      
      
        
          Jan 1, 2026. Anne Marie Green. Working paper.
        
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
        
          Abstract
          
            
              I use machine learning to quantify and describe the scope of symbolic legislation in the U.S., and experimental survey research to understand what symbolic legislation means to voters, and how this meaning differs from material economic policies.
            
          
        
      

      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
    
  
    
      Political Ideology in Campaign Mail 
      
      
      
      
        
          Jun 1, 2025. Josh Kalla, David Broockman, and Anne Marie Green. Working paper.
        
      
      
      
      
      
      
      

      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
    
  
    
      Can a National Domestic Exchange Program “Bridge Divides”? 
      
      
      
      
        
          Jun 1, 2025. Anne Marie Green and Juliana Schroeder. Working paper.
        
      
      
      
      
      
      
      
        
          Abstract
          
            
              Schroeder and I work with a domestic exchange program for high-school students that aims to connect divided Americans by facilitating free, one-week exchanges for teenagers to build shared experiences with other teenagers from different regions and backgrounds. We study the impact of this cross-country travel and connection using longitudinal survey methodology, a between-subjects experiment, and ethnographic methods collected over one week of a summer exchange in Jackson, Georgia.

title: Photos
text:
2026









On a “Stranger Things” tour in Jackson, Georgia.







A bar advertising “Husband Day Care” in Jackson, GA.







Packing summertime lunches at a Methodist Church in Jackson, GA.









Writing cards for the elderly at the same church.







Free Prayer in the square of Covington, Georgia. Also where “The Vampire Diaries” and several clips from movies were filmed.







Learning how to shoot in Jackson, Georgia.
